section.title: Praise the LORD, for He Is Good
section.sectionRationale: A hymn of praise opening Book V's second great block of temple songs, paired liturgically with Psalm 136, weaving creation, the exodus, and the conquest into one call to bless the LORD who chose Jacob and stands utterly above the lifeless idols of the nations.
section.thesis: The LORD alone deserves praise because He is sovereign over creation and history, chose Israel as His treasured possession, and acts, unlike the silent, sightless idols of the nations, so that every house of His people should bless His enduring name.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the call to praise in the temple courts (vv1-4), the LORD's sovereignty over creation and history recalled (vv5-14), the folly of lifeless idols (vv15-18), and a closing chorus of blessing from every house of Israel (vv19-21).

units.label: Praise the name of the LORD
units.note: call to praise in the temple courts

verses.text: Hallelujah! Praise the name of the LORD. Give praise, O servants of the LORD,
who stand in the house of the LORD, in the courts of the house of our God.
Hallelujah, for the LORD is good; sing praises to His name, for it is lovely.
For the LORD has chosen Jacob as His own, Israel as His {{segullah|treasured possession}}.

units.label: The LORD does all that pleases Him

verses.text: For I know that the LORD is great; our Lord is above all gods.
The LORD does all that pleases Him in the heavens and on the earth, in the seas and in all their depths.
He causes the clouds to rise from the ends of the earth. He generates the lightning with the rain and brings forth the wind from His storehouses.
He struck down the firstborn of Egypt, of both man and beast.
He sent signs and wonders into your midst, O Egypt, against Pharaoh and all his servants.
He struck down many nations and slaughtered mighty kings:
Sihon king of the Amorites, Og king of Bashan, and all the kings of Canaan.
He gave their land as an inheritance, as a heritage to His people Israel.
Your name, O LORD, endures forever, Your renown, O LORD, through all generations.
For the LORD will vindicate His people and will have compassion on His servants.
The idols of the nations are silver and gold, made by the hands of men.
They have mouths, but cannot speak; they have eyes, but cannot see;
they have ears, but cannot hear; nor is there breath in their mouths.
Those who make them become like them, as do all who trust in them.
O house of Israel, bless the LORD; O house of Aaron, bless the LORD;
O house of Levi, bless the LORD; you who fear the LORD, bless the LORD!
Blessed be the LORD from Zion, He who dwells in Jerusalem. Hallelujah!

segullah.gloss: A treasured possession, private property especially valued by its owner. God calls Israel His segullah, echoing Exodus 19:5, a term of intimate, chosen belonging rather than mere ownership.

basic.context: A post exilic hymn calling the priests and Levites standing in the temple courts to praise the LORD, recounting His mastery over creation, His mighty acts in the exodus and conquest, and contrasting His living power with the silent idols of the nations.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes Israel's specific redemptive history as grounds for praise, and by that pattern calls every generation to root worship not in vague sentiment but in concrete acts of God, remembered and retold.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel is called God's segullah, a treasured possession, echoing Exodus 19:5. The idols, by contrast, are elilim, worthless things, mere silver and gold shaped by human hands, utterly unlike the LORD who speaks, sees, hears, and acts.
basic.typology: The exodus deliverance and conquest of Canaan's kings foreshadow the greater deliverance and inheritance won in Christ, who defeats every hostile power and gives His people an eternal inheritance, Colossians 2:15, 1 Peter 1:4.
basic.christ: Christ is the true and living Word, unlike the mute idols of verses 16-17; He speaks, sees, hears, and acts decisively in the greater exodus of the cross and resurrection, disarming rulers and authorities, Colossians 2:15, and He is the name that endures forever, Philippians 2:9-11.
basic.god: God is shown as sovereign over nature, active in history's decisive acts of judgment and deliverance, compassionate toward His servants, and utterly incomparable to anything made by human hands.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 19:5 calls Israel God's treasured possession. Jeremiah 10:3-5 mocks idols in nearly identical language. Colossians 2:15 shows Christ disarming rulers and authorities in a greater exodus victory. Revelation 19:1-6 picks up this psalm's Hallelujah chorus in heavenly worship.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's praise for the LORD who chose Israel, ruled creation, and defeated her enemies while idols stood silent, finds its fulfillment in Christ, the living Word who defeats every hostile power and gathers a people, from every nation, as God's own treasured possession.

context.literary: A hymn bookended by 'Hallelujah,' verses 1 and 21, with God's enduring name as its centerpiece, verse 13, moving from temple call to praise, through creation and history, to the folly of idols, and closing with a full chorus of blessing from every house of Israel.
context.historical: Likely composed for temple worship in the post exilic community, echoing earlier Scripture, especially Psalm 115 and Deuteronomy, to root a rebuilt community's identity in the LORD's proven, historical acts rather than in present circumstances.
context.canonical: Closely paired with Psalm 136, both retelling creation and the exodus as grounds for praise; Jewish tradition sang the two together in morning prayer, combining Hallelujah with the refrain 'His loving devotion endures forever.'

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To summon God's people, especially those serving in the temple, to praise the LORD by rehearsing His sovereign acts in creation and history, and to expose the folly of trusting anything less than the living God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes specific historical acts, the exodus plagues, the defeat of Sihon and Og, as grounds for praise; it prescribes as an abiding pattern that worship should be rooted in remembering God's concrete deeds, not in vague feeling.

debates.question: Does verse 14's 'vindicate' point mainly to God's past defense of Israel or to an ongoing pattern of justice for His people?

views.label: Primarily a summary of God's past historical acts of defense for Israel
views.case: The verse sits within a psalm recounting specific past deliverances, the exodus and conquest, suggesting it summarizes what God has already done for His people.

views.label: A statement of God's ongoing, continuing pattern of vindicating and having compassion on His servants
views.case: The verse's language, closely echoing Deuteronomy 32:36, presents vindication as God's settled character and abiding practice, not a one time event.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the God who acted decisively for Israel in the past is the same God who continues to vindicate and show compassion to His servants, grounding present hope in past faithfulness.

study.originalLanguages: Israel is called God's segullah, a treasured possession, echoing Exodus 19:5's language of chosen, intimate belonging. The nations' gods are called elilim, worthless things, mere silver and gold, utterly unlike the LORD who speaks, sees, hears, and acts in history.

typology.type: The exodus deliverance and defeat of Sihon, Og, and the kings of Canaan
typology.fulfillment: Christ's greater exodus at the cross, disarming rulers and authorities and triumphing over them, Colossians 2:15.

typology.type: Israel named God's treasured possession, segullah
typology.fulfillment: The church, gathered from every nation, called a people for God's own possession, 1 Peter 2:9.

study.christ: Christ is the living Word who speaks, sees, hears, and acts, utterly unlike the mute and powerless idols this psalm mocks. In His death and resurrection He accomplishes a greater exodus, disarming the rulers and authorities that once held sway, Colossians 2:15, and He now bears the name above every name, before which every knee will bow, Philippians 2:9-11, fulfilling this psalm's confession that the LORD's name and renown endure through all generations.
study.god: God is shown as utterly sovereign, doing all that pleases Him in the heavens, the earth, and the seas, decisively active in the great turning points of Israel's history, compassionate toward His servants, and incomparable to the silent idols of the nations who can neither speak, see, hear, nor breathe.

crossReferences.note: Names Israel God's treasured possession, the same term this psalm applies to Jacob and Israel in verse 4.
Mocks lifeless idols in strikingly similar language to this psalm's verses 15-18.
Shows Christ disarming rulers and authorities, a greater exodus victory than the one this psalm recounts.
Calls the church a people for God's own possession, extending this psalm's segullah language to believers in Christ.

study.oneStory: This psalm's praise for the LORD who created the world, delivered Israel from Egypt, defeated her enemies, and stood utterly above lifeless idols finds its fulfillment in Christ, the living Word, who wins a greater deliverance at the cross and gathers a people from every nation as God's own treasured possession, forever praising His enduring name.

quiz.q: According to verse 4, why has the LORD chosen Jacob?

options.text: Because Israel is His treasured possession.
options.note: Verse 4 states this directly, echoing Exodus 19:5's language of chosen belonging.

options.text: Because Israel was the strongest nation on earth.
options.note: The psalm never grounds God's choice in Israel's strength or merit.

options.text: Because Israel had no idols at the time.
options.note: The psalm does not give this as the reason for God's choice; it simply names Israel as His treasured possession.

options.text: Because Jacob built the first temple.
options.note: Solomon, not Jacob, built the first temple; this is not the psalm's stated reason.

quiz.q: What specific historical acts does the psalm recall as proof of God's power?

options.text: Striking Egypt's firstborn and defeating Sihon, Og, and the kings of Canaan.
options.note: Verses 8-12 name exactly these events as proof of the LORD's sovereign power in history.

options.text: The building of Solomon's temple.
options.note: The temple's construction is not mentioned in this psalm's historical recital.

options.text: The return from the Babylonian exile.
options.note: While the psalm may date from the post exilic period, the events it recounts are the exodus and conquest, not the return itself.

options.text: The crossing of the Jordan under Joshua alone.
options.note: The psalm's specific historical recital centers on Egypt's plagues and the defeat of named kings, not the Jordan crossing.

quiz.q: How does the psalm describe the idols of the nations?

options.text: Silver and gold, made by human hands, unable to speak, see, or hear.
options.note: Verses 15-17 describe idols in exactly these terms, mocking their powerlessness.

options.text: Powerful spirits that rival the LORD's authority.
options.note: The psalm insists idols have no real power at all, unlike this option suggests.

options.text: Ancient relics no longer worshiped by anyone.
options.note: The psalm addresses idols as actively worshiped and trusted, not as forgotten relics.

options.text: Living creatures created by the LORD for a purpose.
options.note: Idols are described as lifeless objects made by human hands, not living creatures.

quiz.q: What warning does verse 18 give about those who trust in idols?

options.text: They become like the idols they trust, lifeless and unresponsive.
options.note: Verse 18 states this directly: those who make and trust idols become like them.

options.text: They will immediately lose all their possessions.
options.note: The psalm's warning is about spiritual likeness to idols, not material loss specifically.

options.text: They will be physically struck down like Egypt's firstborn.
options.note: That judgment is described earlier in the psalm concerning Egypt, but verse 18's warning is about becoming like lifeless idols.

options.text: Nothing; the psalm gives no consequence for idol worship.
options.note: Verse 18 explicitly states a serious spiritual consequence.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament pick up this psalm's theme of the living God versus lifeless idols?

options.text: Christ, the living Word, defeats hostile powers and is given the name above every name.
options.note: Colossians 2:15 and Philippians 2:9-11 develop exactly this contrast between Christ's living power and the powerlessness this psalm ascribes to idols.

options.text: The New Testament abandons any concern with idolatry.
options.note: The New Testament repeatedly warns against idolatry, continuing this psalm's concern.

options.text: It applies this theme only to first century Roman emperor worship.
options.note: The New Testament's warnings against idolatry are broader than one historical instance.

options.text: It reverses the psalm's teaching, treating idols as equally valid paths to God.
options.note: The New Testament, like this psalm, insists idols are powerless and unworthy of trust, not equally valid.

questions: The psalm calls Israel God's 'treasured possession.' How does knowing you are treasured by God, not merely tolerated, change the way you approach Him in prayer this week?
Verses 8-12 root praise in specific, remembered acts of God in history. What specific things has God actually done in your own life that you could recount today as grounds for praise?
The psalm mocks idols that have mouths but cannot speak, eyes but cannot see. What modern 'idols,' things you look to for security or identity, might be just as powerless as the psalm describes?
Verse 18 warns that those who trust idols become like them, lifeless. Where might you be shaping your own heart after something that cannot truly see, hear, or save you?

The psalm ends with every house, Israel, Aaron, Levi, and 'all who fear the LORD,' called to bless Him together. Who could you invite into worship with you this week who might otherwise praise God alone?

translationNotes.note: Treasured possession renders segullah, a term for private, especially valued property, used of Israel also in Exodus 19:5 and Deuteronomy 7:6.
Vindicate can also be rendered judge, reflecting a Hebrew verb that covers both defending the innocent and rendering righteous verdicts.
This description of idols closely parallels Psalm 115:4-8, suggesting a shared liturgical tradition mocking idol worship across multiple psalms.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via web_fetch from biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/135.htm (Berean Standard Bible), verified against the section map bounds Psalm 135:1-21. No em or en dashes present in the source verses. No verified sermon found this session from the trusted cluster specifically expounding Psalm 135 as its primary text, so sermon status is none.
